fd.title: Fibrous dysplasia — clinical guidelines (living document)
fd.basedOn: Boyce et al. 2019 (PMID 31196103) plus 23 approved update PRs through April 2026
fd.statusBy: Dr. N. Appelman-Dijkstra · Dr. M. Riminucci

sections.title: 1. Diagnosis
sections.intro: FD diagnosis combines imaging, histopathology when biopsy is obtained, and confirmation of a somatic GNAS mutation.

paragraphs.text: First-line imaging for suspected craniofacial FD is non-contrast facial CT with thin slices (≤1 mm). The classic pattern is ground-glass bone with loss of corticomedullary distinction.
MRI with contrast is indicated when soft-tissue compression is suspected (optic nerve, brain, inner ear). MRI complements CT; it does not replace it for bone detail.
Bone scintigraphy once at diagnosis may help define extent (monostotic vs polyostotic). Routine repeat scans are not recommended because of cumulative radiation exposure and limited follow-up value.

sections.title: 2. Histopathology and genetics
sections.intro: Histology alone is insufficient. GNAS confirmation is required when FD is suspected.

paragraphs.text: Classic histology shows irregular trabeculae of woven bone in a fibrous stroma. The pattern may overlap with juvenile trabecular ossifying fibroma, especially in children.

Molecular testing for somatic GNAS (commonly R201H or R201C in exon 8) is pathognomonic when positive. A negative blood test does not exclude mosaic disease — repeat from affected tissue if clinical suspicion remains high.

sections.title: 3. Medical therapy
sections.intro: Treatment depends on pain, functional impact, and skeletal maturity. Conservative management is preferred in asymptomatic children until growth is complete.

paragraphs.text: Observation is standard for asymptomatic patients until skeletal maturity (typically 16–18 years), with clinical review every 6–12 months and imaging every 12–24 months when indicated.
IV bisphosphonates (pamidronate or zoledronate per weight-based protocols) are considered for pain refractory to NSAIDs, with calcium and vitamin D supplementation and renal monitoring.
Denosumab 60 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks until skeletal maturity.

Denosumab 60 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks for six induction doses, then every 12 weeks if CTX remains below 0.3 ng/mL. Monitor calcium and 25-OH vitamin D weekly during induction, then monthly during maintenance.
After denosumab discontinuation, rebound hypercalcemia is a risk — plan a taper and consider transition to bisphosphonates for 6–12 months.
Burosumab is reserved for FGF23-mediated hypophosphatemia (phosphate <0.8 mmol/L with elevated FGF23). Titrate per weight with phosphate monitoring every two weeks during dose adjustment.

sections.title: 4. Surgery
sections.intro: In children, surgery is generally avoided except for strictly defined compelling indications.

paragraphs.text: Prophylactic resection of FD in children is not recommended. Historical series report high recurrence and permanent deformity after aggressive early surgery.
Absolute indications include threatened or established vision loss, symptomatic cranial nerve compression, or airway compromise from deformity.
Pre-symptomatic optic canal decompression based on imaging alone is not recommended — operative risk may exceed risk from observation in asymptomatic patients.

sections.title: 5. Monitoring
sections.intro: Follow-up intensity depends on disease extent, age, and whether MAS overlap features are present.

paragraphs.text: Clinical review every 6 months in children and annually in stable adults, including pain scores, function, growth parameters, and endocrine symptoms when MAS overlap is possible.
Bone turnover markers (P1NP, CTX) every 6 months, or more often on denosumab. Calcium, phosphate, 25-OH vitamin D, and ALP at least annually.
Screen for MAS-related endocrinopathy at FD diagnosis: thyroid function, IGF-1/GH axis, cortisol, and pubertal staging in children.

mas.title: McCune-Albright syndrome — clinical guidelines (living document)
mas.basedOn: Consensus endocrine and skeletal care pathways for MAS

sections.title: 1. Endocrine surveillance
sections.intro: GnRH-independent precocious puberty and other endocrinopathies require age-specific surveillance.

paragraphs.text: Annual Tanner staging in MAS girls aged 2–8.
Tanner staging every 6 months in MAS girls aged 2–7 (peak window for GnRH-independent precocious puberty).

noonan.title: Noonan syndrome — clinical guidelines (living document)
noonan.basedOn: RASopathy cardiac and growth surveillance consensus

sections.title: 1. Cardiology
sections.intro: Pulmonary valve stenosis and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy drive surveillance intensity.

paragraphs.text: Baseline echocardiography at diagnosis and before any pulmonary valve intervention; document gradient, valve morphology, and ventricular function.
